claims.claim_text: High levels of surface wheat residue increased the yield of soybean grown after wheat in weedy plots (20 to 29% increase as residue rose from 0 to 6 Mg/ha) by reducing giant foxtail infestation, but soybean yield was similar across residue levels in weed-free plots.
claims.evidence_text: In absence of frost in 1993 and 1994, yield of weedy soybean increased 20 to 29%, respectively, with the increase of SWR from 0 to 6 Mg ha −1 . In weed-free plots, soybean yield was similar across all SWR levels. These results confirm the hypothesis that high levels of SWR increased soybean yield in weedy plots because of decreased giant foxtail infestation.

context.condition: benefit observed in weedy plots; no effect in weed-free plots
context.note: frost in 1992 injured soybean more in plots with residue

mechanisms: surface wheat residue suppressed giant foxtail (weed) infestation

provenance.source_title: Surface Wheat (Triticum aestivum) Residues, Giant Foxtail (Setaria faberi), and Soybean (Glycine max) Yield | Weed Science | Cambridge Core
